attributes.description: Alerts when the request rate drops to zero (`RATE(apache.requests) == 0`). Unexpected zero traffic usually points to upstream routing / DNS / load-balancer problems.
attributes.name: [Apache OTel] No requests received

investigation_guide.blob:
## Apache no requests received

### What fired
`RATE(apache.requests)` was 0 (or null) across the evaluation window.

### Why it matters
A server that is running but serving zero requests is effectively unavailable. The cause is rarely Apache itself — usually an upstream routing, DNS, or load balancer problem is starving the server of traffic.

### Triage
1. Confirm whether *this* server is unique or the whole fleet is affected (Overview dashboard).
2. Check the load balancer health-check status and target registration.
3. Verify DNS resolution for the virtual host.
4. Inspect external synthetic probes / RUM for a broader outage signal.
5. Look at `apache.uptime` to rule out a recent restart during which no traffic has arrived yet.

### Remediation
- Fix the upstream routing/DNS/load-balancer issue.
- If Apache itself is misconfigured and rejecting traffic, inspect `error_log` for vhost / cert failures.

### Tuning
- Rule fires on any zero-rate sample. If you have maintenance windows, suppress it via tags or a separate maintenance rule.

esqlQuery.esql:
TS metrics-apachereceiver.otel-*
| WHERE apache.requests IS NOT NULL
| STATS request_rate = SUM(RATE(apache.requests))
    BY resource.attributes.apache.server.name
| WHERE request_rate == 0.0 OR request_rate IS NULL
| SORT resource.attributes.apache.server.name ASC
| LIMIT 10
